Diagnosing inotify limits causing dotnet watch crashes

I'm looking at the truncated terminal output to see what happened.


Read the truncated portion of terminal output
The build and tests are passing overall—147 tests succeeded with 41 skipped due to missing Docker and Playwright dependencies—but there's a critical issue: when running the app through run.sh, it starts successfully but then crashes with an inotify error related to dotnet watch.

The problem is that the default inotify instance limit on Linux (128) is being exceeded. I can fix this by setting the DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER environment variable to true, which switches dotnet watch from using inotify to polling instead, completely avoiding the limit issue. The app started successfully, then shut down—likely because the user interrupted it or the script timed out—and when dotnet watch tried to restart and re-establish file watchers, it hit the inotify instance limit. The whole process only took about 6 seconds, so it seems the shutdown happened quickly after startup.

The real issue here is that inotify limit is preventing dotnet watch from working reliably on this Linux system, and I need to configure it to use polling instead. My next steps are to check the dump.txt file for the current source code, locate the implementation plan to see what comes next, fix the inotify problem and any other defects, and then provide the full file paths and downloadable versions.

Architecting sign-in authorization infrastructure and security event recording
Architecting sign-in authorization infrastructure and security event recording

Read run.sh
The issue is that dotnet watch uses inotify for file monitoring, which can hit the system limit on busy Linux machines. Setting the DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER=1 environment variable switches it to polling instead, avoiding the inotify constraint.

The maintainer's documentation frames this as a host configuration issue that should be fixed via sysctl, but since the user wants it treated as a code defect, using the polling watcher is a valid workaround—though it does come with higher CPU overhead on large directory trees.

Looking at the error more carefully, the app actually started successfully before the watch process crashed trying to monitor for changes, so the polling approach would at least keep the development loop running instead of failing immediately. ually, I should be more thoughtful here. The maintainer explicitly flagged this as a host-level issue that needs a sysctl adjustment, so switching to polling in run.sh would be imposing a behavior change with CPU overhead that they might not want. A better approach is to have run.sh attempt to raise the limit itself if it's too low, or at least detect the problem and print clear guidance to the user, while offering polling as an optional environment variable they can enable if needed.

But wait—raising fs.inotify.max_user_instances requires root, and the user is running as a normal user without sudo. So polling is really the only solution that works out of the box without requiring elevated privileges. The core question is whether this is a code defect or a host configuration issue. Given that the user is reporting it as a defect and wants run.sh to simply work, the most pragmatic fix is to enable the polling file watcher by default in run.sh, which ensures the script doesn't crash on any dev machine regardless of the inotify settings. threshold of 128 (the common default) makes sense—I'll warn the user and fall back to polling if the limit is at or below that, which handles the exact issue they're hitting while still respecting systems where the limit is already adequate. Let me code this up. able and >= 256, leave inotify on. Otherwise export DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER=1 and print an info line telling the user why and how to get native watching back (sudo sysctl fs.inotify.max_user_instances=1024 + persist).

Wait — but there's a subtlety: the user has MANY watchers already consuming instances (busy workstation), so even with a limit of, say, 256, they could still exhaust it. But detecting available instances is not straightforward. The limit read is the best proxy we have. I'll keep it simple and document it.

Now — the bigger task: the next step in the implementation plan = "Sign-in + authorization wiring". Let me re-read the "

So if the inotify limit is readable and at least 256, I'll leave native watching enabled; otherwise I'll switch to polling mode and show the user a helpful message with the sysctl command to fix it. I recognize there's a gap here—on a busy workstation with many watchers, even 256 might not be enough—but reading the actual available instances is tricky, so I'll stick with this approach and document the limitation.

Now I need to move on to the next phase of the implementation: wiring up sign-in and authorization. The claims principal factory handles role-to-claims conversion through the user role store, and I need to set up area authorization policies for each endpoint: /table for any authenticated user, /kitchen and /counter for their respective roles plus administrators, /administration for administrators only, with /display/{table} and /display/pair deferred to the device authentication phase. I'm also checking the schema for where security events like sign-in success/failure and account lockouts get recorded, and I need to wire up the sign_ins_total metric. The infrastructure work here is really about plumbing—registering the SignInManager, setting up cookie authentication, stamp validation, authorization policies, and security event recording—but none of it gets exercised without actual sign-in pages, which are part of a later slice. So I need to figure out what's a coherent, testable unit for this first slice: the security_event table structure and the basic recording mechanism. Authorization policies will be defined as constants and registered in the DI container, with enforcement happening via [Authorize(Policy=...)] attributes on components and route-based policy mapping in Blazor areas. I'll wire up app.UseAuthentication() and app.UseAuthorization() in Program.cs in the correct pipeline order, register the security event log service, and add unit tests for the authorization policy logic. I need to provide complete file contents for all the changes with their paths, and I'll create them as downloadable artifacts using the computer. Let me examine the rest of the schema to locate the security_event table and understand the full identity-related structure, then review the existing test patterns in the codebase to match the style.

Architected authentication stack with security event logging and inotify fallback
Architected authentication stack with security event logging and inotify fallback

Read IDatabaseConnectionFactory

Read Sha256Hashing helper and ObligationsPipelineTests

Read ObligationsPipelineTests to match unit test patterns
I've got a solid grasp of the codebase and requirements now, so I'm ready to map out the implementation strategy. M1 is complete, and for M2 I've got identity persistence and Argon2id hashing in place with the core wiring set up—though I still need to add SignInManager, cookie authentication, and proper security measures.

The next phase focuses on sign-in and authorization: I need to wire up SignInManager with secure cookies (HttpOnly, SameSite=Lax, 24-hour sliding window), configure security-stamp revalidation every 5 minutes, map roles to claims through the identity claims factory, set up area-based authorization policies, record sign-in and lockout events to the security event table, and track sign-in metrics by method and result. I also need to address an inotify robustness issue in the dev script—checking the system's inotify limit and enabling polling file watcher if needed without requiring root access.

The build succeeded with 147 tests passing and 41 skipped, so the M2 identity code compiled cleanly with no defects there. Now I'm checking whether AddSignInManager requires explicit authentication middleware setup like AddAuthentication().AddIdentityCookies() or if it's handled automatically by the identity registration. IAuthenticationSchemeProvider and other authentication dependencies come from AddAuthentication. The key insight is that UserClaimsPrincipalFactory<TUser> automatically includes role claims if the user store implements IUserRoleStore<TUser>, so even without explicitly calling AddRoles, role claims will be added to the principal as long as the store supports roles. long as the store implements IUserRoleStore. Since our DapperUserStore does implement that interface, role claims will automatically flow through without needing AddRoles<TRole> — which makes sense since we're using string-based roles rather than a separate role entity.

For the authentication and cookie setup, I'm configuring the standard modern approach with AddAuthentication to set the default schemes and then AddIdentityCookies() to handle the cookie configuration...

Now I need to think through whether AddHttpContextAccessor() is necessary. SignInManager depends on it, and while Blazor Server with interactive components might not register it by default, I should add it to be safe since the cookie infrastructure and stamp validation need access to the HTTP context.

Looking at the two main patterns: Pattern A uses the full AddIdentity with roles, which I don't need since roles are just strings. Pattern B is the composable approach—AddIdentityCore plus manual cookie authentication plus AddSignInManager—which gives me exactly what I need without the role overhead.

For Pattern B, I'm setting up authentication with AddAuthentication to configure the default schemes, then using AddIdentityCookies() to handle the cookie configuration...

Actually, AddIdentityCookies() does more than just set up the ApplicationScheme cookie—it also wires in the SecurityStampValidator through the OnValidatePrincipal event. The validator itself gets registered as ISecurityStampValidator → SecurityStampValidator<TUser>, though that registration depends on whether AddIdentityCore or AddSignInManager has already been called. So AddIdentity registers the security stamp validators and SignInManager, while AddIdentityCore only handles the core user management pieces like UserManager and password validation—it skips the authentication cookies and sign-in logic entirely.

When using AddIdentityCookies(), it wires up the application cookie to validate the security stamp on each request, which means we absolutely need ISecurityStampValidator registered in the container. This is the key constraint that determines how we wire everything together.

Writing the correct service registration... I'm checking the ASP.NET Core source to see if AddSignInManager() automatically registers the security stamp validators, and it looks like it doesn't—so I'll need to register those separately.

The SecurityStampValidator<TUser> constructor requires IOptions<SecurityStampValidatorOptions>, SignInManager<TUser>, and ILoggerFactory, all of which are available, and I can configure the validation interval to 5 minutes through the options.

Now I need to handle the TimeProvider requirement — in .NET 8+, the framework removed the ISystemClock dependency from SecurityStampValidator<TUser> in favor of using TimeProvider from the options or identity configuration.

I'm also realizing that SignInManager<TUser> requires IHttpContextAccessor, which Blazor Server doesn't register by default even with AddRazorComponents().AddInteractiveServerComponents(). Since the cookie authentication handler and SignInManager both need it, I should explicitly add services.AddHttpContextAccessor() to be safe.

There's an important caveat though: SignInManager.SignInAsync and PasswordSignInAsync write to HTTP response cookies, which only works during actual HTTP requests—not during interactive circuit renders where the response has already been sent. This means the sign-in form handling needs to happen on a static SSR endpoint or plain endpoint, not an interactive component event. That's a concern for the sign-in pages slice, not this one. For now I'm just wiring up the services and middleware.

Now I'm setting up the AddRestaurantIdentity method, configuring HTTP context access, authentication schemes with identity cookies, the identity core with a custom Dapper user store, token providers, and sign-in manager, plus the security stamp and two-factor validators.

Configuring application cookie... ConfigureApplicationCookie will post-configure the application cookie that AddIdentityCookies already registered, so that's the right approach. Now I'm setting up authorization policies by creating constants for role names like Administrator, Kitchen, and Counter, along with policy name constants for table and kitchen access.

Defining authorization policies... I'm realizing there's a potential issue with how the RoleClaimType gets preserved when the authentication cookie is deserialized. The cookie stores the serialized claims and the identity's role claim type configuration, so when it's reconstructed during validation, those settings should carry through correctly.

Now I'm checking whether the roles themselves are being normalized properly. The user manager retrieves roles from the store in lowercase, so the role claims end up lowercase too. When RequireRole is called with a constant like Administrator = "administrator", it should match against those lowercase claims. But I need to verify whether the claims factory does any normalization of the role strings themselves. Now I'm thinking through the security event recording piece. I need to define the event types as a closed set that matches the database constraints—either as an enum or constants. Since the domain already has an Authentication namespace, I could put a SecurityEventType there with string values that align with the DB. But I'm weighing whether the recorder abstraction belongs in Domain or DataAccess, and I'm leaning toward putting the event type constants in Domain since they're pure and testable, while the actual recording logic would live in DataAccess. queried by the admin service. I'm defining a SecurityEventType in the Domain layer as a static class with constant string fields that match the database CHECK constraint, plus a read-only set of all valid types for validation and testing—this keeps the representation simple and faithful to what the database enforces.

For persistence, I'll create an ISecurityEventLog interface in DataAccess with a method to record events asynchronously, taking the subject identifier, optional actor identifier, event type, and cancellation token. The implementation using Dapper will live in DataAccess alongside DapperUserStore, keeping all security-event persistence logic together. The web layer can depend on this interface for recording sign-in events.

Though I'm second-guessing whether the interface should live in Domain instead, since IDomainEventBroadcaster is defined there—I want consistency in where the app codes against interfaces. Defining the implementation classes... Handlers and the admin service are both scoped, so scoped registration is the right choice. Since there are no sign-in pages in this slice yet, I need to figure out where to centrally record sign-in outcomes like succeeded, failed, and locked out events. The cleanest approach is to create a custom SignInManager<Person> subclass that intercepts sign-in results and logs them consistently, regardless of which endpoint triggers the sign-in flow. The security event log interface and Dapper implementation will be the foundation, tested through integration. I'll create a custom RestaurantSignInManager that overrides PasswordSignInAsync to automatically record success, failure, and lockout events along with the metric at the natural framework points, making the recording transparent to any caller. password, bool lockoutOnFailure)— virtual, along with the two-factor and recovery code sign-in methods, plus the baseSignInAsyncthat handles the actual cookie creation. For this particular piece, I'll create a customRestaurantSignInManager` subclass to keep the implementation focused and avoid duplicating framework logic.

Now I'm thinking through the password sign-in override — when it succeeds, I record a sign_in_succeeded event with a metric, and when the account locks out, that's a distinct account_locked_out event. The tricky part is handling both the user overload and the username overload, since the base implementation routes the username version through FindByNameAsync first, then calls the user version. If I only override the user overload, I'll capture the subject correctly, but I need to make sure the username path still flows through properly.

The real constraint is that security_event rows require a subject person, so when someone tries to sign in with a nonexistent username, the base returns SignInResult.Failed without a user object — meaning I can't record a security event (the subject is NOT NULL in the schema). That's acceptable since we only track events for real accounts. The metric for failed sign-ins can still be incremented regardless, since metrics don't need a subject. So I'll split the responsibility: metrics get recorded centrally in the custom SignInManager for all outcomes, while security events only get recorded when I have an actual user to reference.

For a clean implementation, I'm overriding the password sign-in method to wrap the base call and then record both the metric and security event based on the result — succeeded attempts log both, while locked-out attempts just increment the metric. locked out, I should still count it as a failed sign-in attempt for the metric while also emitting the distinct account_locked_out event to capture that specific condition. The RestaurantMetrics contract specifies result values of succeeded or failed, so I'll stick with that constraint and use the event to preserve the lockout signal separately.

For a plain failure like an incorrect password, I'll record the failed metric and the sign_in_failed event. When the result is RequiresTwoFactor, that's an intermediate state—the password was correct but TOTP verification is still pending, so the sign-in isn't actually complete yet. I shouldn't record a terminal outcome at the password step; instead, I'll let the TOTP step determine the final result once two-factor authentication completes.

For the 2FA overrides, when they succeed, I'll record the password success metric and sign_in_succeeded event (since TOTP is part of the password authentication flow). For recovery codes, I also need to capture the recovery_code_used event as specified. I'll be conservative and focus on recording the terminal sign-in outcomes—succeeded, failed, account locked—while leaving recovery code and TOTP events to their respective feature slices with a TODO note. The real question is whether a custom SignInManager is over-engineering when there are no pages exercising it yet, but it's the right architectural place for this logic and it's unit-testable with fakes, so I'll proceed with it.

The plan explicitly calls for sign-in and lockout security events in this slice, so I'll build it. But testing a SignInManager subclass directly requires heavy framework setup—UserManager, HttpContext, claims factory, options—which makes unit tests flaky and brittle. Instead, I'll extract the core decision logic into a pure function in the Domain layer that maps a SignInResult and method to the appropriate event type and metric result, unit-test that exhaustively, then have the SignInManager simply call that pure function alongside the recorder and metric. This keeps the logic testable and aligns with the repo's pattern of putting pure decisions in Domain.

Defining the enum and pure function... Adding more switch cases...

I'm thinking through whether I actually need the SignInManager subclass for this particular slice, or if I should defer that wiring to the next phase and just focus on delivering the pure domain logic, tests, and metric mappings now. Wiring up the constructor...

The logger type is tricky — the base expects ILogger<SignInManager<TUser>>, so I'll inject ILogger<SignInManager<Person>> directly and pass it through, letting DI handle the resolution. For overrides, I need to cover the password and 2FA methods, though the username variant chains to the user-based overload anyway, so I'll focus on the core ones.

Looking at how the base class works, the username overload finds the user by name then delegates to the user overload, which means my override catches both paths for existing users. But when a user doesn't exist, the username path returns Failed immediately without hitting my override — so no event or metric gets recorded for unknown-user attempts. I need to handle that case separately to capture the metric.

I could override both overloads, but that risks double-recording if the username version calls base and triggers the user version. The cleaner approach is to override just the user overload and the 2FA methods, accepting that unknown-username failures won't be metered. Though actually, those failures are worth tracking for credential stuffing detection, so I should override the username overload too to record the metric on that early return path.

Writing the override implementation... Writing the override methods...

I'm handling the two-factor sign-in cases where I need to retrieve the user from the 2FA cookie after the base method completes, then record the authentication attempt if the user is still available. There's a timing consideration here — on successful sign-in, the base method clears the 2FA cookie, so the user might already be null by the time I try to fetch it, but I still want to log the result either way. I shouldn't override SignInAsync directly since it's called internally by the password flow and would cause double-recording. The passkey recording will happen separately in the passkey slice with an explicit method="passkey" parameter. Now I'm building the RecordAsync helper that maps SignInResult to SignInAttemptResult and determines the event type and metric result.

Writing helper methods... Writing the INSERT statement... Dapper handles nullable Guid parameters correctly by converting null to DBNull, and the CHECK constraint on event_type ensures only valid strings are stored—my client-side validation with IsKnown prevents hitting that constraint. Now I'm defining the SecurityEventType domain class with all the event type constants and a collection of valid values. All the required namespaces are already available through existing package references—Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity, Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.Cookies, and Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization are part of the ASP.NET Core shared framework included with the Web SDK, and DataAccess already has Dapper and the necessary Domain dependencies for the security event log. Now I need to update Program.cs to wire up authentication middleware and configure the cookie options. Registering the security event log in AddRestaurantIdentity keeps the identity wiring cohesive, and it'll be available app-wide for the admin service to use later when handling grant/revoke and reset operations.

For the lifetime, since all three dependencies are singletons and the service holds no per-request state, I'll register it as a singleton — there's no reason to allocate it per request, and injecting a singleton into scoped services is safe.

Now I'm setting up the wiring test to verify that SignInManager<Person> resolves correctly as RestaurantSignInManager and that the application cookie is properly configured. Resolving SignInManager<Person> pulls in IHttpContextAccessor, IUserClaimsPrincipalFactory, IAuthenticationSchemeProvider, IUserConfirmation, and logger dependencies—all of which get registered either through AddIdentityCore or the AddAuthentication().AddIdentityCookies() call in AddRestaurantIdentity. The cookie options themselves depend on IOptions<IdentityOptions>, which should also resolve from the identity setup.

For the test, I'll create a helper that builds a minimal service collection mirroring the relevant registrations from Program.cs, calls AddRestaurantIdentity with test options, and then builds the provider to verify everything wires up correctly. Setting up the service collection... policies via provider.GetRequiredService<IAuthorizationPolicyProvider>() and then calling GetPolicyAsync(...) to retrieve them. For the Kitchen policy, I can check that it contains a RolesAuthorizationRequirement with AllowedRoles set to {"kitchen","administrator"}, and verify it also has a DenyAnonymousAuthorizationRequirement for authenticated users. I need to confirm the required types are available from the right namespaces before finalizing this test approach.

For the fake connection factory, I'll implement IDatabaseConnectionFactory to return a connection that throws if called, since the wiring test shouldn't actually open any database connections.

The security event log integration test lives in DataAccess.Tests and uses the PostgreSQL fixture with migrations. I'll create a person to satisfy the foreign key, then test recording events with null actors, with actual actors, and verify that unknown event types throw ArgumentOutOfRangeException on the client side without hitting the database.

For the unknown-type check, I'll make it a separate [Fact] that doesn't require the container—I can construct the log with a fake factory that throws, since the guard validates before touching the database anyway. This gives me a test that always runs.

I should also add a SecurityEventTypeTests unit test in Domain.Tests to verify the All collection has 19 entries, that IsKnown works correctly, and that the constants match what's in the database.

Now I'm revisiting the inotify fix for run.sh. I'll add a comment block before the exec dotnet watch command explaining that dotnet watch creates multiple inotify instances, which can exceed the kernel's per-user limit on busy workstations and crash the watcher. The fix detects if the limit is low and the user hasn't explicitly chosen a watcher, then falls back to polling mode so development still works without needing root access. The native watcher is faster and uses less CPU, but users can restore it by raising the kernel limit with a sysctl command.

Then I'm checking the inotify limit from the system, validating it's a number, and if it's below 256 and no explicit watcher preference is set, I enable the polling file watcher as a fallback.

Wait, I need to clarify whether that 128 value the user saw is the actual limit or their current usage — that's an important distinction for whether this fix would actually help.

The error message says "The configured user limit (128) ... has been reached," so 128 is definitely the limit itself. My check for < 256 would catch this and enable polling, which should work around the issue.

The trade-off with polling is that it adds some CPU overhead from periodically scanning the directory tree, but for a project this size it's negligible and worth it to avoid needing root access. Though I'm wondering if I should tighten the threshold to <= 128 since that's the known-bad default — but then again, someone with a 128 limit who hasn't hit exhaustion wouldn't benefit from the slower reload times polling introduces.

I think < 256 is actually the right call: it catches the common 128 default that triggered this issue, while letting anyone who's already bumped their limit to 256 or higher keep native file watching. Plus, users can always force native watching by setting DOTNET_USE_POLLING_FILE_WATCHER=0, which my check respects since it looks for an unset variable.

Raising the sysctl limit automatically would require root and prompt for a password, which would break the user's non-interactive workflow, so the polling fallback is the better approach. Now I need to update the BUILD_PROGRESS.md file to mark the inotify issue as handled with the polling fallback solution, move the completed slice to done, and update the remaining task list.

Since this is implementing the existing spec rather than changing it, I don't need to update the normative spec — just the execution tracker. Before I write the authentication code, let me verify there are no compile risks with the .NET 10 Identity APIs I'm planning to use.

AddIdentityCookies is available through the DI extensions in Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity, accessible since .NET 5, so that's safe. AddSignInManager<RestaurantSignInManager>() is also available as an extension on IdentityBuilder. Now I'm checking the remaining dependencies like ISecurityStampValidator... I need to register both validators since the cookie validation callbacks will resolve them at runtime — ISecurityStampValidator for the main cookie and ITwoFactorSecurityStampValidator for the two-factor cookie. Now I'm looking at configuring the application cookie settings. The policy configuration takes an Action<AuthorizationPolicyBuilder> delegate, and I can use RequireRole to specify which roles are allowed. For the cookie settings, CookieSecurePolicy.Always and SameSiteMode.Lax are both available in Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http. Now I need to verify the exact constructor signature for RestaurantSignInManager : SignInManager<Person> in net10.

The base SignInManager constructor takes seven parameters: the user manager, HTTP context accessor, claims principal factory, identity options, logger, authentication scheme provider, and user confirmation service. I'll mirror that signature and inject my custom dependencies—the security event log and restaurant metrics—passing the base parameters up to the parent constructor. Then I'll override the password sign-in method to add my custom logic. I can access UserManager as a public property on SignInManager to call FindByNameAsync. SignInResult has the properties I need (Succeeded, IsLockedOut, IsNotAllowed, RequiresTwoFactor) plus a static Failed method. One thing to verify: whether FindByNameAsync expects the normalized username.

Actually, UserManager.FindByNameAsync normalizes the input internally before querying the store, and since the base PasswordSignInAsync uses the same method, my approach is consistent. For SecurityEventType.All, I'll initialize it as an IReadOnlySet<string> with a collection expression since the domain targets net10. The ISecurityEventLog.RecordAsync method returns a Task.

Nullable annotations are complete throughout. Adding AddAuthentication won't conflict with the existing AddDataProtection setup — they work together naturally, with the cookie authentication leveraging Data Protection to encrypt tickets, which aligns with the existing key persistence to the volume. The RestaurantSignInManager depends on RestaurantMetrics and ISecurityEventLog, both of which are resolvable, and it's registered as scoped.

Now I need to verify whether AddSignInManager<RestaurantSignInManager>() registers both the concrete type and the SignInManager<Person> interface mapping. Let me trace through the extension method...

Checking the registration logic... Writing the registration logic...

So both SignInManager<Person> and RestaurantSignInManager resolve to the same instance, which is why my test passes. I'm realizing that AddIdentityCore doesn't register SignInManager on its own—only AddSignInManager does—and since I'm only calling my custom AddSignInManager<RestaurantSignInManager>() once without also calling the parameterless version, there shouldn't be any conflicts.

The auth stack is in place with AddAuthentication().AddIdentityCookies(), so resolving SignInManager within a test scope should work fine. I'm keeping AddDefaultTokenProviders() because I'll need the TOTP provider for 2FA later. And AddIdentityCore<Person> already registers IUserConfirmation<Person> as DefaultUserConfirmation<Person>, so SignInManager's constructor dependency resolves without issues.

Since RequireConfirmedAccount is false, the confirmation gate in CanSignInAsync won't block anything. Now I'm verifying the registration order in AddRestaurantIdentity makes sense: HTTP context accessor first, then authentication with identity cookies, authorization builder, identity core with the custom sign-in manager and stores, and finally the stamp validators registered as scoped.

Then I need to configure the application cookie (which post-configures what AddIdentityCookies set up, so it has to come after), set the security stamp validator options, replace the password hasher with the custom implementation, and register the security event log. The order of these later steps is flexible for DI, but ConfigureApplicationCookie specifically needs to follow AddIdentityCookies. I'll keep the existing Replace call for the password hasher since that's already in place.

I'm realizing that AddAuthorizationBuilder() returns a builder for chaining policies and also registers the authorization services internally, so I don't need a separate AddAuthorization() call. It handles setting up the defaults too. For the areas, I can't attach [Authorize] attributes to components that don't exist yet, but I can set up the route-level authorization conventions now so they're ready when the pages are created.

Actually, I should defer the router configuration and AuthorizeRouteView setup to when the actual area pages are built in later slices — applying those attributes to concrete components is premature right now and would affect the Home page behavior. What I should do in this slice is define and register the named authorization policies themselves, which is exactly what the plan calls for. The actual application of those policies to specific area pages happens when those pages are implemented. So defining the policies now is the right scope for this slice.

But I'm also wondering whether to add CascadingAuthenticationState wrapping around the router in Routes.razor and register AddCascadingAuthenticationState() so that auth state flows to components. It's low-risk wiring that future-proofs the app without breaking anything, but it might be better left for when there's actually something that needs it. To keep this slice focused and avoid unnecessary changes to Routes.razor, I'll hold off on that for now.

The middleware pipeline with UseAuthentication and UseAuthorization is fine even without any authorized endpoints yet — it'll just populate HttpContext.User from the cookie and enforce nothing, leaving the health endpoints and Home anonymous. No regression there.

I'm also checking whether AddAuthentication().AddIdentityCookies() could conflict with anything from AddRazorComponents in a Blazor Server app — it won't, since Blazor doesn't add auth on its own. And I need to verify the metric result label choice doesn't violate anything with RestaurantMetrics.RecordSignI...

I'm setting up the DataAccess integration test for DapperSecurityEventLog, which requires a PostgreSQL fixture with migrations and test data. I need to create both a subject person and an actor person — I could use DapperUserStore directly, but that introduces a dependency on DataProtectionProvider, so inserting the person rows directly via Dapper is simpler and more isolated.

The test class will implement IClassFixture<PostgreSqlFixture> and IAsyncLifetime, with an InsertPersonAsync helper to create test people, then instantiate the log with a connection factory, clock, and UUID identifier factory. I'll write three test cases: one recording a sign-in event with no actor, another recording a role grant with both subject and actor, and a third verifying that an unknown event type throws ArgumentOutOfRangeException.

For the unknown-type test, I'll use a throwing connection factory so the guard check fails before any database access happens. Since the fixture's InitializeAsync catches container startup failures and sets SkipReason without throwing, the test class still constructs and non-gated tests run, making this a reliable always-run validation.

I'm pulling in the dependencies: IIdentifierFactory and UuidV7IdentifierFactory from the domain identifiers namespace, IClock from domain time, and FixedClock from the test utilities. For verification, I'll query the security_event table to check the event type and actor person identifier, handling nullable UUIDs with QuerySingleAsync<Guid?>.

Now I need to verify the timestamp round-trip — Postgres stores timestamptz as a UTC instant, and Npgsql converts it to a DateTimeOffset with offset 0. Since my fixed clock is set to 2026-01-15 12:00:00 +00:00 and timestamptz has microsecond precision, the equality check should hold cleanly. I'll generate unique usernames for each test to prevent collisions.

For the security_event foreign key, I need to ensure the subject person exists first. My insert statement supplies the required non-nullable columns — person_identifier, username, security_stamp, and created_at — while the other columns use their defaults or remain null as appropriate.

Now I'm writing the integration test.

Done
Now the DataAccess integration test for the security-event log (skips without a container; the guard test always runs).


DataAccess integration test for DapperSecurityEventLog against real PostgreSQL
DataAccess integration test for DapperSecurityEventLog against real PostgreSQL

DataAccess integration test for DapperSecurityEventLog against real PostgreSQL
I'm wondering whether Dapper can map directly to value tuples by column position—it does support tuple mapping, but typically relies on name matching for named tuples, which can be unreliable. For unnamed tuples in recent versions, it should map by position, but I need to verify this works for the specific case of mapping to (string, Guid?, DateTimeOffset).

To avoid potential mapping bugs, I'm leaning toward a safer approach: either making separate scalar queries for each column, or defining a private class with properties that match the column aliases. Since the store uses PascalCase aliases and Dapper matches property names case-insensitively but doesn't automatically convert snake_case to PascalCase without the MatchNamesWithUnderscores setting, I'll need to ensure the column aliases align with the property names.

I'll rewrite ReadSingleEventAsync using a private class paired with explicitly aliased columns—this keeps it consistent with the codebase pattern and avoids any mapping surprises.

Now I'm setting up the WebApplication wiring test, which means I need to construct a service collection with all the dependencies that AddRestaurantIdentity expects—logging, metrics, the clock abstraction, identifier factory, a fake database connection factory, and data protection—then invoke the method and verify the wiring is correct.

The assertions I need to make are: SignInManager<Person> resolves to RestaurantSignInManager, the application cookie options are configured properly, the security stamp validator has a 5-minute validation interval, and the authorization policies are registered with the right requirements.

To resolve SignInManager<Person>, I need to trace through its dependencies: RestaurantSignInManager requires a UserManager<Person>, HTTP context accessor, claims principal factory, identity options, logger, authentication scheme provider, user confirmation service, security event log, and metrics. The UserManager in turn needs a user store (which will be DapperUserStore), identity options, password hasher (Argon2id with metrics), and other standard components.

All these dependencies should be registered through AddIdentityCore, the custom AddRestaurantIdentity call, AddDataProtection, AddAuthentication, and the test setup, so resolving within a scope should work.

I'm deciding whether to use validateScopes: true when building the service provider — if I do, I'll need to resolve scoped services within a scope rather than at the root. I could also use validateOnBuild: true to catch registration issues upfront, but that might fail on open-generic registrations. I'll stick with the default BuildServiceProvider() and just resolve within a scope to be safe.

For the authorization components, IAuthorizationPolicyProvider is registered as transient by AddAuthorization, so it's resolvable from the root. Now I need to check what GetPolicyAsync does.

Cookie options come from IOptionsMonitor<CookieAuthenticationOptions> as a singleton, and I'll retrieve them using the application scheme. The security stamp validator options are also a singleton via IOptions<SecurityStampValidatorOptions>, where I can access the validation interval.

When asserting the kitchen policy requirements, I'm checking that it contains a RolesAuthorizationRequirement with allowed roles for kitchen and administrator staff, plus a DenyAnonymousAuthorizationRequirement. I'll verify the allowed roles set matches what's expected.

Now I'm setting up the test itself — building a RestaurantOptions inline and registering a fake IDatabaseConnectionFactory. I'll need to add the necessary using directives for the authorization infrastructure types. Adding custom domain dependencies... The cookie options are registered as named options per scheme through AddIdentityCookies, so I need to check how IOptionsMonitor<CookieAuthenticationOptions> resolves them.

I'm doing a manual review of the C# code for correctness since I can't compile it without the net10 SDK. I'm focusing on the trickiest parts, starting with the RestaurantSignInManager base constructor signature.

I'm verifying that the seven parameters I'm passing to the base SignInManager constructor match the canonical .NET signature exactly, and confirming that I'm adding two additional parameters for my subclass. The authentication scheme provider interface comes from Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication, which I have imported. Now I need to check if I have the using statement for IHttpContextAccessor from Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http in my RestaurantSignInManager file. The Web SDK's implicit usings cover Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http, so IHttpContextAccessor resolves without an explicit using statement. Since the existing code already relies on implicit usings for types like WebApplication and ILogger, I'll keep it consistent and not add the using.

Now I'm checking the IdentityServiceCollectionExtensions to verify its dependencies — AddAuthentication comes from the implicit Web usings, and I need to trace through what AddIdentity requires. Confirming the namespaces for the remaining dependencies — AddIdentityCore<Person> comes from the identity services extensions, and I'm verifying that services.AddRestaurantAuthorization() is properly referenced from my custom authorization namespace. Checking the remaining dependency injection registrations — AddDefaultTokenProviders, AddSignInManager<RestaurantSignInManager>, and TryAddScoped<ISecurityStampValidator, SecurityStampValidator<Person>> all resolve correctly from their respective namespaces, and I've already got the necessary usings in place. ConfigureApplicationCookie is an extension method on IServiceCollection that lives in the Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection namespace as part of the Identity extensions, so I need to make sure I'm pulling from the right namespace for that method.

Now I'm checking the types used in the configuration: CookieSecurePolicy and SameSiteMode both come from Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http, which is implicitly available in the Web SDK, and CookieAuthenticationOptions comes from Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.Cookies which I already have imported. I'm also setting up the Configure<SecurityStampValidatorOptions> call on services.

Configure, which uses the extension method from Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection and the options type from Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity. Finally, I'm registering a custom IPasswordHasher<Person> as a singleton and using services.Replace to swap it in, both of which are available through the dependency injection extensions.

Now I'm verifying the namespaces for the remaining registrations: ISecurityEventLog and DapperSecurityEventLog are both in MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Identity, which I'm already using. I notice the file imports Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.Extensions, but I should check if that's actually needed. The original file also uses these same types without explicit imports, so that's consistent. TimeSpan comes from System implicitly. Now I'm checking AuthorizationPolicies.cs — it needs Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization for the authorization builder and policy methods, which are extension methods on IServiceCollection. AddAuthorizationBuilder is an extension method from Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection, so it's implicitly available. Since I'm not referencing any types from Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization by name—only using builder instance methods and string constants—that using directive is unnecessary and will trigger an IDE0005 warning, though it won't be treated as an error with the current build settings. Good, so IServiceCollection and AddAuthorizationBuilder are available through the implicit usings in the Web SDK project. I'll remove that unused authorization using statement.

Now looking at SecurityEventType.cs in the Domain layer — it uses IReadOnlySet<string>, HashSet<string>, and StringComparer.Ordinal. Since it's a standard class library with implicit usings enabled, System and System.Collections.Generic are already available, so those types should resolve fine.

SignInAudit.cs also looks good — it just references SecurityEventType from the same namespace and uses switch expressions, no explicit usings needed.

For ISecurityEventLog.cs in DataAccess, it only references SecurityEventType through an XML doc comment cref attribute. Since documentation generation is disabled in the project settings, the cref won't be validated at build time, so even if the reference isn't fully resolvable, it won't cause a compilation error. System.Threading is also covered by implicit usings, so all the types I need (Task, CancellationToken, Guid) are available without explicit imports. Now I'm checking the DapperSecurityEventLog file, which needs System.Data.Common for database connections, Dapper for the query execution, and references to the domain authentication types. so child namespaces can access parent namespace types without an explicit using statement — C# resolves outward through the namespace hierarchy. Since DapperUserStore.cs is in MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Identity, it can see IDatabaseConnectionFactory from the parent MyRestaurant.DataAccess namespace directly, so no additional using is needed.

Looking at the usings in DapperUserStore — System.Data.Common, Dapper, Microsoft.AspNetCore.DataProtection, Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity, and the domain namespaces — no explicit using MyRestaurant.DataAccess; is there, yet it references IDatabaseConnectionFactory in the constructor. This confirms the parent namespace resolution is working as expected. DapperSecurityEventLog follows the same pattern.

Dapper's ExecuteAsync extension on DbConnection and CommandDefinition are both available through the Dapper using, so that's covered.

Now looking at DapperSecurityEventLogTests.cs — it imports NpgsqlDatabaseConnectionFactory, SchemaMigrationRunner, PostgreSqlFixture, and FixedClock from their respective namespaces in the DataAccess layer and tests infrastructure. Tests) is also visible. So all the types I need are accessible through namespace resolution rules. So DapperSecurityEventLoglives inMyRestaurant.DataAccess.Identity, which is a sibling namespace to my test's .Identityfolder, and I've correctly added that using to access it.  ForSecurityEventTypeandUuidV7IdentifierFactory, I have the right usings. The using MyRestaurant.Domain.Time;is questionable since I'm usingFixedClockdirectly (which is in the same test namespace) rather than referencingIClockby name, so that import might be unnecessary.UuidV7IdentifierFactorycomes fromMyRestaurant.Domain.Identifiersso that using is needed.Guid.CreateVersion7()is available in net10, andTestContext.Current.CancellationTokenis from xunit v3 which I'm already using.ThrowingConnectionFactoryimplementsIDatabaseConnectionFactoryfrom theMyRestaurant.DataAccessnamespace, so that's all good. I'll remove the unusedusing MyRestaurant.Domain.Time;` to clean things up.

Now looking at SecurityEventTests.cs in Domain.Tests — it imports MyRestaurant.Domain.Authentication and Xunit, using SecurityEventType with LINQ's OrderBy. The SDK has implicit usings including System.Linq, so that's covered. The test uses Assert.Equal to compare two IEnumerable<string> sequences, which xunit handles correctly.

Moving to SignInAuditTests.cs — it also uses Enum.GetValues<SignInAttemptResult>() from System, working with SecurityEventType, SignInAudit, and SignInAttemptResult. The [InlineData] attributes pass SecurityEventType.SignInSucceeded (a const string) and enum values, both of which are valid in attributes.

Now checking IdentityWiringTests.cs in WebApplication.Tests — it's a Microsoft.NET.Sdk test project. need to verify I have all the required using statements for the dependencies — DependencyInjection, AspNetCore.Http, DataProtection, Identity, and Authentication.Cookies are all accounted for. Checking the extension methods for AddLogging(), AddMetrics(), and AddDataProtection() — they're all in Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection, though I need to verify whether AddDataProtection() comes from Microsoft.AspNetCore.DataProtection or the extensions namespace. AddDataProtection() is definitely in Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection, so I should remove the Microsoft.AspNetCore.DataProtection using to avoid an unused-using warning. IDE0005 warnings do run during build with EnforceCodeStyleInBuild=true, but they won't fail the build, so keeping the using is fine. Now I need to verify that the test properly resolves IAuthorizationPolicyProvider — AddRestaurantIdentity chains into AddRestaurantAuthorization which calls AddAuthorizationBuilder(), and that should register all the necessary authorization services including the policy provider.

The DefaultAuthorizationPolicyProvider needs IOptions<AuthorizationOptions>, which AddAuthorization registers, so that dependency chain works. The provider is registered as transient and resolvable from the root container. When I call GetPolicyAsync, it returns a Task<AuthorizationPolicy?>, and I'm asserting on the RolesAuthorizationRequirement.AllowedRoles collection by ordering them and comparing against the expected values.

For the Table policy, RequireAuthenticatedUser() adds a DenyAnonymousAuthorizationRequirement, but since .RequireRole isn't called, there's no RolesAuthorizationRequirement to find. Now I'm verifying that SignInManager<Person> resolves correctly in a scope by checking all its transitive dependencies — UserManager<Person> needs validators and a lookup normalizer, which AddIdentityCore provides by default. IUserClaimsPrincipalFactory<Person> needs UserManager<Person> and IOptions<IdentityOptions>, both available. IUserConfirmation<Person> maps to DefaultUserConfirmation<Person>. IAuthenticationSchemeProvider comes from AddAuthentication. SignInManager<Person> (the restaurant variant) depends on ISecurityEventLog via DapperSecurityEventLog, which needs the fake database factory, clock, and identifier factory—all registered. RestaurantMetrics is there too. Everything chains together, though I need to verify how DapperUserStore gets registered through AddUserStore.

RestaurantSignInManager is scoped, so creating a scope in the test is correct. The default BuildServiceProvider() doesn't validate scopes or build-time validation, so resolving scoped dependencies from the root container won't throw an error—but I'm already using a scope where needed.

For the namespace collision concern, Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization doesn't define an AuthorizationPolicies type, so there's no conflict with my custom AuthorizationPolicies class from the MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Authorization namespace.

Now I'm ready to apply the fixes. Checking the Web SDK's implicit usings to confirm whether Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization is included by default — if it's not in the implicit list, then the using is genuinely unused and should be removed. On second thought, keeping both usings is the safer approach—an extra using just triggers a warning, but a missing one causes a compile error. I'll keep Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization in AuthorizationPolicies.cs and MyRestaurant.Domain.Time in DapperSecurityEventLogTests.cs to avoid any risk of breaking the build. I'm confident AddAuthorizationBuilder is a DI extension method, so removing Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization from AuthorizationPolicies.cs is safe. Similarly, I don't use IClock in DapperSecurityEventLogTests.cs, so removing that using is also safe. Both removals keep the code clean without any correctness risk, so I'll make both edits now.

I should add RecoveryCodeUsed event recording to the recovery-code sign-in path. It's straightforward to emit this alongside the sign-in success audit, either by passing it as an additional event parameter or recording it inline right after the sign-in audit completes. Adding the recovery code event logging... I'm reconsidering whether adding the recovery code event directly in the SignInManager is the cleanest approach, since it blurs the separation between pure decision logic and side effects. But it's a legitimate consequence of that specific override path, so I'll keep it focused and well-documented.

This completes the Milestone 2 "Sign-in + authorization wiring" slice: SignInManager, Identity cookie auth (Secure/HttpOnly/SameSite=Lax, 24 h sliding), security-stamp revalidation every 5 min, roles→claims, the four area authorization policies, and sign-in/lockout security-event logging + the sign_ins_total{method,result} metric. Strategized batch retrieval for remaining files
Strategized batch retrieval for remaining files
1. src/MyRestaurant.Domain/Authentication/SecurityEventType.cs (new)

csharp
namespace MyRestaurant.Domain.Authentication;

/// <summary>
/// The closed vocabulary of security-audit event types (TECHNICAL_SPECIFICATION §3.4–§3.7). These
/// strings are the exact values allowed by the <c>security_event.event_type</c> CHECK constraint
/// (§8.2); keeping them in one BCL-only place lets the web layer raise events, the data layer persist
/// them, and a unit test assert the set matches the schema — all without duplicating string literals.
///
/// <para>Do not rename a value without a coordinated migration: the database rejects any string not in
/// this list, so a typo here surfaces as a CHECK violation at write time rather than a compile error.
/// <see cref="IsKnown"/> gives callers a cheap client-side guard so a bad value fails fast with a clear
/// exception instead of a raw PostgreSQL error.</para>
/// </summary>
public static class SecurityEventType
{
    // Account lifecycle (§3.7).
    public const string AccountCreated = "account_created";
    public const string AccountDeactivated = "account_deactivated";
    public const string AccountReactivated = "account_reactivated";

    // Passwords (§3.2, §3.5, §3.7).
    public const string PasswordChanged = "password_changed";
    public const string PasswordResetByAdministrator = "password_reset_by_administrator";
    public const string ForcedPasswordChangeCompleted = "forced_password_change_completed";

    // TOTP + recovery codes (§3.4, §3.5, §3.7).
    public const string TotpEnrolled = "totp_enrolled";
    public const string TotpRemoved = "totp_removed";
    public const string TotpClearedByAdministrator = "totp_cleared_by_administrator";
    public const string ForcedTotpEnrollmentCompleted = "forced_totp_enrollment_completed";
    public const string RecoveryCodeUsed = "recovery_code_used";
    public const string RecoveryCodesRegenerated = "recovery_codes_regenerated";

    // Passkeys (§3.3).
    public const string PasskeyRegistered = "passkey_registered";
    public const string PasskeyRemoved = "passkey_removed";

    // Roles (§3.6, §3.7).
    public const string RoleGranted = "role_granted";
    public const string RoleRevoked = "role_revoked";

    // Sign-in outcomes (§3.5). The metric sign_ins_total{method,result} mirrors these (§12).
    public const string SignInSucceeded = "sign_in_succeeded";
    public const string SignInFailed = "sign_in_failed";
    public const string AccountLockedOut = "account_locked_out";

    /// <summary>Every valid <c>event_type</c>, in the order the schema lists them.</summary>
    public static readonly IReadOnlySet<string> All = new HashSet<string>(StringComparer.Ordinal)
    {
        AccountCreated,
        AccountDeactivated,
        AccountReactivated,
        PasswordChanged,
        PasswordResetByAdministrator,
        ForcedPasswordChangeCompleted,
        TotpEnrolled,
        TotpRemoved,
        TotpClearedByAdministrator,
        ForcedTotpEnrollmentCompleted,
        RecoveryCodeUsed,
        RecoveryCodesRegenerated,
        PasskeyRegistered,
        PasskeyRemoved,
        RoleGranted,
        RoleRevoked,
        SignInSucceeded,
        SignInFailed,
        AccountLockedOut,
    };

    /// <summary>True when <paramref name="eventType"/> is one the schema will accept (case-sensitive).</summary>
    public static bool IsKnown(string eventType) => All.Contains(eventType);
}

Read SignInAudit.cs for inline backup
Read SignInAudit.cs for inline backup
2. src/MyRestaurant.Domain/Authentication/SignInAudit.cs (new)

csharp
namespace MyRestaurant.Domain.Authentication;

/// <summary>
/// The terminal-ish outcome of a single sign-in attempt, distilled from ASP.NET Core Identity's
/// <c>SignInResult</c> into a BCL-only value the domain can reason about. The web layer maps the
/// framework result onto this; the domain decides what to audit. Keeping the decision here (rather
/// than inside the <c>SignInManager</c> subclass) makes it exhaustively unit-testable, exactly as the
/// obligations pipeline is (TECHNICAL_SPECIFICATION §3.5, §16.1).
/// </summary>
public enum SignInAttemptResult
{
    /// <summary>Fully authenticated — the cookie was issued.</summary>
    Succeeded,

    /// <summary>Credentials were rejected (wrong password, wrong/absent second factor).</summary>
    Failed,

    /// <summary>The account is locked out (either already, or this attempt tripped the threshold, §3.1).</summary>
    LockedOut,

    /// <summary>The password was correct but a second factor is still required — not yet a terminal outcome.</summary>
    RequiresTwoFactor,

    /// <summary>Sign-in is not permitted for this account (e.g. a confirmation gate, unused here).</summary>
    NotAllowed,
}

/// <summary>
/// The pure audit decision for a sign-in attempt (TECHNICAL_SPECIFICATION §3.5, §12): given the
/// outcome, which <see cref="SecurityEventType"/> row (if any) should be written, and which
/// <c>result</c> label the <c>sign_ins_total</c> metric should carry (if any).
///
/// <para><see cref="SignInAttemptResult.RequiresTwoFactor"/> is deliberately silent on both: the
/// password step passing is not a completed sign-in, so the terminal event/metric is recorded by the
/// second-factor step instead. Everything else is terminal and is both audited and metered.</para>
/// </summary>
public static class SignInAudit
{
    /// <summary>
    /// The security-event type to persist for <paramref name="result"/>, or <c>null</c> when nothing
    /// should be recorded yet (the two-factor challenge case).
    /// </summary>
    public static string? SecurityEventFor(SignInAttemptResult result) => result switch
    {
        SignInAttemptResult.Succeeded => SecurityEventType.SignInSucceeded,
        SignInAttemptResult.LockedOut => SecurityEventType.AccountLockedOut,
        SignInAttemptResult.Failed => SecurityEventType.SignInFailed,
        SignInAttemptResult.NotAllowed => SecurityEventType.SignInFailed,
        SignInAttemptResult.RequiresTwoFactor => null,
        _ => null,
    };

    /// <summary>
    /// The <c>result</c> tag value for the <c>sign_ins_total{method,result}</c> metric (§12), or
    /// <c>null</c> when the attempt should not be metered yet (the two-factor challenge case). A
    /// lockout is metered as a failure while being audited as its own <c>account_locked_out</c> event.
    /// </summary>
    public static string? MetricResultFor(SignInAttemptResult result) => result switch
    {
        SignInAttemptResult.Succeeded => MetricSucceeded,
        SignInAttemptResult.Failed => MetricFailed,
        SignInAttemptResult.LockedOut => MetricFailed,
        SignInAttemptResult.NotAllowed => MetricFailed,
        SignInAttemptResult.RequiresTwoFactor => null,
        _ => null,
    };

    /// <summary>The <c>result</c> tag value recorded on a successful sign-in.</summary>
    public const string MetricSucceeded = "succeeded";

    /// <summary>The <c>result</c> tag value recorded on any non-success terminal outcome.</summary>
    public const string MetricFailed = "failed";
}

Read ISecurityEventLog.cs for inline backup
Read ISecurityEventLog.cs for inline backup
3. src/MyRestaurant.DataAccess/Identity/ISecurityEventLog.cs (new)

csharp
namespace MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Identity;

/// <summary>
/// Appends rows to the <c>security_event</c> table (TECHNICAL_SPECIFICATION §8.2). Security events are
/// an audit trail, never mutated and never deleted: sign-in outcomes and lockouts today (§3.5), and
/// administrative actions — resets, grants/revocations, deactivations — as those land in later M2
/// slices (§3.7). It lives beside the Dapper Identity store because it is pure persistence; callers in
/// the web layer depend on this interface rather than on SQL.
/// </summary>
public interface ISecurityEventLog
{
    /// <summary>
    /// Records one security event.
    /// </summary>
    /// <param name="subjectPersonIdentifier">
    /// The account the event is about (the <c>security_event.subject_person_identifier</c>; NOT NULL).
    /// </param>
    /// <param name="actorPersonIdentifier">
    /// The person who caused it, or <c>null</c> when the subject acted on themselves or the system did
    /// (e.g. a sign-in attempt, a lockout). Maps to the nullable <c>actor_person_identifier</c>.
    /// </param>
    /// <param name="eventType">
    /// One of <see cref="MyRestaurant.Domain.Authentication.SecurityEventType"/> — validated before the
    /// write so an unknown value fails fast rather than as a raw CHECK violation.
    /// </param>
    /// <param name="cancellationToken">Cancels the write.</param>
    Task RecordAsync(
        Guid subjectPersonIdentifier,
        Guid? actorPersonIdentifier,
        string eventType,
        CancellationToken cancellationToken = default);
}

Read DapperSecurityEventLog.cs for inline backup
Read DapperSecurityEventLog.cs for inline backup
4. src/MyRestaurant.DataAccess/Identity/DapperSecurityEventLog.cs (new)

csharp
using System.Data.Common;
using Dapper;
using MyRestaurant.Domain.Authentication;
using MyRestaurant.Domain.Identifiers;
using MyRestaurant.Domain.Time;

namespace MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Identity;

/// <summary>
/// The Dapper-backed <see cref="ISecurityEventLog"/> over the <c>security_event</c> table
/// (TECHNICAL_SPECIFICATION §8.2). Like <see cref="DapperUserStore"/> it holds no connection: each call
/// opens one from the injected factory and disposes it, so a single instance is safe for the scoped
/// Identity lifetime. Identifiers are application-generated UUIDv7 (ADR-0011); timestamps come from the
/// injected <see cref="IClock"/> so tests are deterministic.
/// </summary>
public sealed class DapperSecurityEventLog : ISecurityEventLog
{
    private readonly IDatabaseConnectionFactory _connectionFactory;
    private readonly IClock _clock;
    private readonly IIdentifierFactory _identifierFactory;

    public DapperSecurityEventLog(
        IDatabaseConnectionFactory connectionFactory,
        IClock clock,
        IIdentifierFactory identifierFactory)
    {
        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(connectionFactory);
        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(clock);
        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(identifierFactory);

        _connectionFactory = connectionFactory;
        _clock = clock;
        _identifierFactory = identifierFactory;
    }

    public async Task RecordAsync(
        Guid subjectPersonIdentifier,
        Guid? actorPersonIdentifier,
        string eventType,
        CancellationToken cancellationToken = default)
    {
        ArgumentException.ThrowIfNullOrEmpty(eventType);

        // Fail fast with a clear message: the column has a CHECK for exactly this set, so a bad value
        // would otherwise surface as an opaque PostgreSQL CheckViolation at write time.
        if (!SecurityEventType.IsKnown(eventType))
        {
            throw new ArgumentOutOfRangeException(
                nameof(eventType),
                eventType,
                "Unknown security event type; it must be one of MyRestaurant.Domain.Authentication.SecurityEventType.");
        }

        // Cast the nullable actor to uuid so Npgsql resolves the parameter type whether the value is a
        // Guid or NULL (mirrors the '::citext' casts in DapperUserStore). The subject is always present.
        const string sql = """
            INSERT INTO security_event (
                security_event_identifier, subject_person_identifier, actor_person_identifier,
                event_type, occurred_at)
            VALUES (
                @SecurityEventIdentifier, @SubjectPersonIdentifier, @ActorPersonIdentifier::uuid,
                @EventType, @OccurredAt);
            """;

        await using DbConnection connection = await _connectionFactory
            .OpenConnectionAsync(cancellationToken)
            .ConfigureAwait(false);

        await connection.ExecuteAsync(new CommandDefinition(
            sql,
            new
            {
                SecurityEventIdentifier = _identifierFactory.Create(),
                SubjectPersonIdentifier = subjectPersonIdentifier,
                ActorPersonIdentifier = actorPersonIdentifier,
                EventType = eventType,
                OccurredAt = _clock.UtcNow,
            },
            cancellationToken: cancellationToken)).ConfigureAwait(false);
    }
}

Read AuthorizationPolicies.cs for inline backup
Read AuthorizationPolicies.cs for inline backup
5. src/MyRestaurant.WebApplication/Authorization/AuthorizationPolicies.cs (new)

csharp
namespace MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Authorization;

/// <summary>
/// The three stored role names (TECHNICAL_SPECIFICATION §3.7), spelled exactly as the
/// <c>person_role.role_name</c> CHECK stores them — lower case. The Dapper role store returns roles in
/// this casing, the claims factory turns each into a role claim verbatim, and the policies below match
/// on it, so the whole chain agrees on one spelling. <c>guest</c> is not here: it is the implicit
/// capacity of any authenticated person on their own order, never a stored role. <c>table_display</c>
/// is a device principal, not a person role.
/// </summary>
public static class RestaurantRoles
{
    public const string Administrator = "administrator";
    public const string Kitchen = "kitchen";
    public const string Counter = "counter";
}

/// <summary>
/// Named authorization policies for the routed areas (TECHNICAL_SPECIFICATION §3.7). The policies are
/// registered here; pages attach them with <c>[Authorize(Policy = ...)]</c> as each area is built in
/// later milestones. The display-device policies (<c>/display/{table}</c>, <c>/display/pair</c>) are a
/// device-principal concern and arrive with M3.
/// </summary>
public static class AuthorizationPolicies
{
    /// <summary><c>/table</c> — any authenticated person (per-sitting membership is checked separately).</summary>
    public const string Table = "area.table";

    /// <summary><c>/kitchen</c> — the kitchen or administrator role.</summary>
    public const string Kitchen = "area.kitchen";

    /// <summary><c>/counter</c> — the counter or administrator role.</summary>
    public const string Counter = "area.counter";

    /// <summary><c>/administration</c> — the administrator role.</summary>
    public const string Administration = "area.administration";
}

/// <summary>Registers the area authorization policies (§3.7).</summary>
public static class AuthorizationServiceCollectionExtensions
{
    public static IServiceCollection AddRestaurantAuthorization(this IServiceCollection services)
    {
        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(services);

        services.AddAuthorizationBuilder()
            .AddPolicy(AuthorizationPolicies.Table, policy => policy
                .RequireAuthenticatedUser())
            .AddPolicy(AuthorizationPolicies.Kitchen, policy => policy
                .RequireAuthenticatedUser()
                .RequireRole(RestaurantRoles.Kitchen, RestaurantRoles.Administrator))
            .AddPolicy(AuthorizationPolicies.Counter, policy => policy
                .RequireAuthenticatedUser()
                .RequireRole(RestaurantRoles.Counter, RestaurantRoles.Administrator))
            .AddPolicy(AuthorizationPolicies.Administration, policy => policy
                .RequireAuthenticatedUser()
                .RequireRole(RestaurantRoles.Administrator));

        return services;
    }
}

Read RestaurantSignInManager.cs for inline backup
Read RestaurantSignInManager.cs for inline backup
6. src/MyRestaurant.WebApplication/Identity/RestaurantSignInManager.cs (new)

csharp
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity;
using Microsoft.Extensions.Options;
using MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Identity;
using MyRestaurant.Domain.Authentication;
using MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Observability;

namespace MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Identity;

/// <summary>
/// A <see cref="SignInManager{TUser}"/> that audits every sign-in outcome centrally
/// (TECHNICAL_SPECIFICATION §3.5, §12): whichever endpoint drives the sign-in, the terminal result is
/// recorded once as a <c>security_event</c> and once on the <c>sign_ins_total{method,result}</c>
/// metric. The <em>decision</em> of what to record lives in the pure <see cref="SignInAudit"/>
/// (unit-tested exhaustively); this class only maps the framework's <see cref="SignInResult"/> onto it
/// and performs the side effects.
///
/// <para>Only the password and second-factor paths are overridden here. The passkey path
/// (<c>SignInManager.SignInAsync</c> after a WebAuthn assertion, method = <c>passkey</c>) records its
/// own success in the passkey slice, so <see cref="SignInManager{TUser}.SignInAsync(TUser,bool,string)"/>
/// is intentionally not overridden — overriding it would double-count the password path, which calls it
/// internally on success.</para>
///
/// <para>A subject is required for a <c>security_event</c> row (the column is NOT NULL, §8.2), so a
/// failed attempt against a non-existent username is metered but not audited — there is no account to
/// attribute it to. <c>RequiresTwoFactor</c> is neither audited nor metered here: the password step
/// passing is not a completed sign-in, and the second-factor step records the terminal outcome.</para>
/// </summary>
public sealed class RestaurantSignInManager : SignInManager<Person>
{
    /// <summary>The <c>method</c> tag for the password path, including its TOTP/recovery second factor (§3.5).</summary>
    private const string PasswordMethod = "password";

    private readonly ISecurityEventLog _securityEventLog;
    private readonly RestaurantMetrics _metrics;

    public RestaurantSignInManager(
        UserManager<Person> userManager,
        IHttpContextAccessor contextAccessor,
        IUserClaimsPrincipalFactory<Person> claimsFactory,
        IOptions<IdentityOptions> optionsAccessor,
        ILogger<SignInManager<Person>> logger,
        IAuthenticationSchemeProvider schemes,
        IUserConfirmation<Person> confirmation,
        ISecurityEventLog securityEventLog,
        RestaurantMetrics metrics)
        : base(userManager, contextAccessor, claimsFactory, optionsAccessor, logger, schemes, confirmation)
    {
        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(securityEventLog);
        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(metrics);

        _securityEventLog = securityEventLog;
        _metrics = metrics;
    }

    /// <summary>
    /// Username + password. Reimplements the base's "resolve the user, else fail" step so that a
    /// failure against an unknown username is still metered, then routes real users through the
    /// user-typed overload below (which does the auditing) — no double counting.
    /// </summary>
    public override async Task<SignInResult> PasswordSignInAsync(
        string userName,
        string password,
        bool isPersistent,
        bool lockoutOnFailure)
    {
        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(userName);

        Person? user = await UserManager.FindByNameAsync(userName).ConfigureAwait(false);
        if (user is null)
        {
            // No account to attribute a security_event to; still meter the failed attempt (§12).
            RecordMetric(SignInAttemptResult.Failed);
            return SignInResult.Failed;
        }

        return await PasswordSignInAsync(user, password, isPersistent, lockoutOnFailure).ConfigureAwait(false);
    }

    /// <summary>Known user + password: the base does the work; we audit the terminal outcome.</summary>
    public override async Task<SignInResult> PasswordSignInAsync(
        Person user,
        string password,
        bool isPersistent,
        bool lockoutOnFailure)
    {
        SignInResult result = await base
            .PasswordSignInAsync(user, password, isPersistent, lockoutOnFailure)
            .ConfigureAwait(false);

        await AuditAsync(user, result).ConfigureAwait(false);
        return result;
    }

    /// <summary>The TOTP second factor on the password path (§3.5): audit the terminal outcome.</summary>
    public override async Task<SignInResult> TwoFactorAuthenticatorSignInAsync(
        string code,
        bool isPersistent,
        bool rememberClient)
    {
        // Capture the pending user before the base runs: a successful sign-in clears the two-factor
        // cookie, after which GetTwoFactorAuthenticationUserAsync would return null.
        Person? user = await GetTwoFactorAuthenticationUserAsync().ConfigureAwait(false);
        SignInResult result = await base
            .TwoFactorAuthenticatorSignInAsync(code, isPersistent, rememberClient)
            .ConfigureAwait(false);

        await AuditAsync(user, result).ConfigureAwait(false);
        return result;
    }

    /// <summary>
    /// A recovery code standing in for the TOTP second factor (§3.4): audit the sign-in outcome, and —
    /// on success — also record that a recovery code was consumed. The base only signs in when the code
    /// was actually redeemed, so a succeeded result means exactly one code was spent.
    /// </summary>
    public override async Task<SignInResult> TwoFactorRecoveryCodeSignInAsync(string recoveryCode)
    {
        Person? user = await GetTwoFactorAuthenticationUserAsync().ConfigureAwait(false);
        SignInResult result = await base
            .TwoFactorRecoveryCodeSignInAsync(recoveryCode)
            .ConfigureAwait(false);

        await AuditAsync(user, result).ConfigureAwait(false);

        if (result.Succeeded && user is not null)
        {
            await _securityEventLog
                .RecordAsync(user.PersonIdentifier, actorPersonIdentifier: null, SecurityEventType.RecoveryCodeUsed)
                .ConfigureAwait(false);
        }

        return result;
    }

    // ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    /// <summary>Meters the attempt and, when there is a subject, records the security event.</summary>
    private async Task AuditAsync(Person? user, SignInResult result)
    {
        SignInAttemptResult attempt = Classify(result);
        RecordMetric(attempt);

        if (user is null)
        {
            return;
        }

        string? eventType = SignInAudit.SecurityEventFor(attempt);
        if (eventType is not null)
        {
            // The subject acted on themselves / the system observed the attempt — no distinct actor.
            await _securityEventLog
                .RecordAsync(user.PersonIdentifier, actorPersonIdentifier: null, eventType)
                .ConfigureAwait(false);
        }
    }

    private void RecordMetric(SignInAttemptResult attempt)
    {
        string? metricResult = SignInAudit.MetricResultFor(attempt);
        if (metricResult is not null)
        {
            _metrics.RecordSignIn(PasswordMethod, metricResult);
        }
    }

    private static SignInAttemptResult Classify(SignInResult result)
    {
        if (result.Succeeded)
        {
            return SignInAttemptResult.Succeeded;
        }

        if (result.IsLockedOut)
        {
            return SignInAttemptResult.LockedOut;
        }

        if (result.IsNotAllowed)
        {
            return SignInAttemptResult.NotAllowed;
        }

        if (result.RequiresTwoFactor)
        {
            return SignInAttemptResult.RequiresTwoFactor;
        }

        return SignInAttemptResult.Failed;
    }
}

Read IdentityServiceCollectionExtensions.cs for inline backup
Read IdentityServiceCollectionExtensions.cs for inline backup
7. src/MyRestaurant.WebApplication/Identity/IdentityServiceCollectionExtensions.cs (changed — full rewrite)

csharp
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.Cookies;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity;
using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection.Extensions;
using MyRestaurant.DataAccess.Identity;
using MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Authorization;
using MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Configuration;
using MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Observability;

namespace MyRestaurant.WebApplication.Identity;

/// <summary>
/// Wires ASP.NET Core Identity <b>core</b> services (never the EF default stores/UI) over the custom
/// Dapper store, with the Argon2id hasher replacing Identity's PBKDF2 default
/// (TECHNICAL_SPECIFICATION §3.1–§3.2, ADR-0003/ADR-0008), plus sign-in and authorization:
/// <list type="bullet">
///   <item>the Identity cookie scheme, hardened (Secure, HttpOnly, SameSite=Lax, 24-hour sliding);</item>
///   <item><see cref="RestaurantSignInManager"/> (the auditing <see cref="SignInManager{TUser}"/>);</item>
///   <item>security-stamp revalidation every 5 minutes, so resets, role revocations, and
///   deactivations bite live sessions within minutes (§3.1);</item>
///   <item>the area authorization policies (§3.7), via <see cref="AuthorizationServiceCollectionExtensions"/>;</item>
///   <item>the append-only <see cref="ISecurityEventLog"/> that sign-in outcomes are recorded to (§3.5).</item>
/// </list>
///
/// Roles flow to claims automatically: the store implements <see cref="IUserRoleStore{TUser}"/>, so the
/// default claims-principal factory adds a role claim per granted role at sign-in — no role entity or
/// <c>RoleManager</c> is registered (roles are plain strings, §3.7). The sign-in <em>pages</em> and the
/// obligations-pipeline middleware are the next M2 slices; this method wires the services they need.
/// </summary>
public static class IdentityServiceCollectionExtensions
{
    /// <summary>The application authentication cookie name.</summary>
